data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

2048 GMT - The DC-based Computer & Communications Industry Association applauds President Trump's decision to terminate talks with Canada on a tariff-resolution deal amid Ottawa's decision to press ahead with a digital-services tax. Trump's decision underscores that U.S. digital commerce "must be treated fairly, and that taxes aimed at U.S. companies in violation of existing trade commitments will face consequences," says Matt Schruers, the association's CEO. The tech lobby had pressed the White House for action against Canada's digital tax, a 3% levy on revenue that is retroactive to 2022. Canada formally implemented the levy last year, and U.S. officials had warned for some time about trade repercussions unless it ditched the levy. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1829 GMT - Canada's economy is dealt another big blow with President Trump's move to end trade talks with Ottawa, citing Canada's imposition of a digital-services tax and complaints over Canada's dairy system. GDP data from earlier in the day indicated Canada's economy shrank in April, and Statistics Canada says another drop in output is expected in the May report. Trade uncertainty has cast a pall on hiring and investment decisions in Canada, and economists fear that weakness to date concentrated in trade-exposed sectors like manufacturing are spreading. A rupture in US-Canada talks, and the possibility of indefinite tariffs and perhaps more levies at hefty rates, poses more headaches for the Bank of Canada as it juggles a weakening economy and inflation threats. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1820 GMT - Canada's digital services tax is at the heart of President Trump's shock move to end all trade talks with Canada to resolve the current trade conflict. Canada had ample warnings from Washington over the past year or so that proceeding with the levy carried consequences. The Biden administration, senior Congressional leaders, and in recent months Trump have warned Canada that the levy had to go, or else Canada could face stiff retaliation. In his post, Trump says Canada's digital levy is an "egregious tax," and represents a "direct and blatant attack" on the US. This week, the DC-based Computer & Communications Industry Association called on the White House to launch a formal trade investigation into Canada's digital levy. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1654 GMT - As the AI trade comes back into favor two tech stalwarts are closing in on the $4 trillion market cap mark: Nvidia and Microsoft. Wedbush says the next milestone might not be far behind. "We believe both Nvidia and Microsoft will hit the $4 trillion market cap club this summer and then over the next 18 months the focus will be on the $5 trillion club....as this tech bull market is still early being led by the AI Revolution," the analysts write. They add that other cloud/hyperscale players such as Alphabet and Amazon are also finding major AI momentum. They say "the impact of the AI cycle on consumer Internet will be massive," as Amazon's AWS and Alphabet's Google Cloud Platform acquire AI-capable chips, build AI-capable service offerings, and sell those services into their respective installed bases. (patrick.sheridan@wsj.com)

1555 GMT - The workhorse of the AI sector and the S&P rally, Nvidia, is up nearly 2% today and has rallied more than 60% since early April when it hit a low following President Trump's global tariff announcement. Analysts say the AI trade is back on after stumbling early this year when Chinese firm DeepSeek said it developed an AI model at a fraction of competitors' costs. But analysts now say strong earnings from tech companies bolster the case that demand for advanced chips is still high as U.S. companies prepare for an AI dominated future. Tariffs also seem like less of a threat, with the White House backing off from some tariffs as it looks to secure trade deals. That's a good sign for chip makers, that rely on demand from auto makers, manufacturers, electronics makers, etc. (patrick.sheridan@wsj.com)

1439 GMT - Tariff-linked challenges are likely to cause global growth to slow. This would increase the risk premia on European stocks, Bank of America strategists say in a note. Bank of America has a negative view on European equities which is "predicated on the expectation of weakening global growth." The strategists have an underweight recommendation on European cyclical stocks and a marketweight recommendation on European utilities. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1301 GMT - Airbnb has begun ramping up its offering of Services within its new Experiences initiative, D.A. Davidson analyst Tom White says. The short-term rental platform expanded to start offering travelers experiences like tours and classes, and Experiences listings have increased 20% since November. The new Services category within Experiences is still small since it launched in May, with about 34 Service listings per city, per White's analysis. If Experiences and Services grow quickly, White believes Airbnb could have a 5% to 10% revenue share of the global online tours, activities and experiences market. That would represent an additional $750 million to $1.5 billion in net revenue for Airbnb. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1002 GMT - AI and the yuan's internationalization could be themes driving the Hang Seng Index's medium-term recovery, Pepperstone strategist Dilin Wu says in a research note. China has adopted a "low-power, high-efficiency" approach to AI development, as startups such as DeepSeek and tech giants including Alibaba and Tencent accelerate AI asset integration and commercialization, she says. "This evolving AI ecosystem fundamentally supports the investment logic for Hong Kong stocks," Wu says. Meanwhile, Beijing is envisioning a bigger role for the yuan on the global stage, including the launch of an operation center for the digital yuan. Hong Kong is a front line for the internationalization of the yuan, and stands to benefit first from digital currency pilots, she adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)
